text: CHAVANTES, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, a tributary of the Araguaya, into which it flows, on the r. bank, near the N point of the island of Bananal, after a NNW course of about 150 m., through a territory chiefly inhabited by an Indian tribe of the same name.

CHAVES, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Tras-os-Montes, comarca and 46 m. W of Braganza, on the r. bank of the Tamega, which is here crossed by a bridge of 16 arches. It is a town of considerable importance, and is defended by three forts. Pop. 5,200. It was the Aquæ Flaviæ of the Romans, and still possesses mineral saline baths of some celebrity, and a few remains of Roman times. Alt. above sealevel 1,230 ft.
CHAVES, or VILLA-DE-EQUADOR, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, on the N coast of the island of Marajo, 120 m. NW of Para.
CHAVES. See Anna-di-Chaves.
CHAVEYRIAT, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Chatillon-les- Dombes. Pop. 2,636.
CHAVIGNAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. and 4 m. WNW of Mauriac. Pop. 1,276.
CHAVIGNOL, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Cher, com. of Sancerre. Pop. 500.
CHAVIGNON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Vailly. Pop. 966.
CHAVILLE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Serres. Pop. 1,562.

CHAVIN, a town of Peru, in the dep. of Jumu, on an affluent of the Chavinella, or Lauricocha, 20 m. N of Caxatambo.—Also another town in the same dep., near the r. bank of the Chavinella, and 40 m. NNE of Caxatambo.
CHAVORNAY, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, on the Orbe river, and 2 m. from Orbe. Pop. 717.
CHAWLEY, or CHAWLEIGH, a parish in Devonshire, 2 m. SSE of Chumleigh. Area 5,020 acres. Pop. 850.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Cumnor, in Berks. Pop. 94.
CHAWPARRAH, a town of Hindostan, in Gundwana, on the Wyne-gunga, 25 m. N of Seuni.
CHAWSON, a hamlet in the p. of Rawton, in Bedfordshire. Pop. 195.
CHAWTON, a parish in Hampshire, 1 m. SSW of Alton. Area 2,870 acres. Pop. 460.
CHAY-CHING, a town of China, in the prov. of Honan, division of Kwei-tih-fu.

CHAYAN-PUR, a district of Nepal, in the Gorkha dominions, extending from the Mechi river, which separates it from Sikkim on the E, to the Arun on ~ / / / / / / / / / / / / / / / / / / / the W. It has an area of about 4,800 sq. m.; and consists altogether of lofty mountains, shooting up in many quarters to a great elevation. Its cap., of the same name, is in N lat. 27° 18', E long. 87°, 114 m. N of Purneah. It has a considerable trade with Tibet; exporting rice, wheat, maraya, uya, oil, butter, iron, copper, cotton cloth, broad cloths, catechu, myrobalans, dhupi planks, pepper, spices, indigo, tobacco, hides, otters' fur, sugar, sugar-candy, and pearls; and receiving in return salt, gold, silver, musk, musk-deer skins, chaungris tails, blankets, borax, Chinese silks, and medicinal herbs.

CHAY-FANG, or TCHE-FANG, a town of China, on the l. bank of the Lung-tchu-en, or Moyen river, in the prov. of Yun-nan, in the division and 40 m. SSE of Tsanta, and 60 m. E of Bhanmo in the Annamese dominions.

CHAYLARD (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, arrond. of Tournon. The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,837.—The com. and town are on the l. bank of the Dorne, 30 m. SW of Tournon. Pop. 2,353. Silkmills and numerous tanneries are established here.
CHAZAY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Anse, 8 m. S of Villefranche. Pop. 717.
CHAZELLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente, cant. of La Rochefoucauld. Pop. 1,025.
CHAZE'-SUR-ARGOS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Cande, 1 m. S of Sagre. Pop. 1,854.
CHAZELLES-SUR-LYON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Saint-Galmier. Pop. 3,001.
CHAZEY-SUR-AIN, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Lagnieu. Pop. 1,120.

CHAZY, a township in Clinton county, in the state of New York, 178 m. N of Albany, on Lake Champlain, watered by the Little Chazy river. Pop. 3,534.—Also a river in Clinton co., which, after a course of 50 m., falls into Lake Champlain.

CHEADLE, a parish and village of Cheshire, 7 m. SSE of Manchester. The p. has an area of 6,470 acres. Pop. 10,145. The Manchester and Birmingham railway intersects this p. and at Cheadle-Hulm sends off a branch to Macclesfield.—Also a p. and market-town in Staffordshire. Area of p. 5,730 acres. Pop. 4,399. The town is situated on the Team, 9 m. E by S of Stoke-upon-Trent. Tape is largely manufactured here. The church is an ancient and interesting edifice; and there is a very handsome Roman Catholic chapel here.
CHEADLE-BULKELEY, a township in the p. of Cheadle, in Cheshire. Area 2,100 acres. Pop. 5,463.
CHEADLE-MOSELEY, a township in the p. of Cheadle, in Cheshire. Area 2,350 acres. Pop. 2,288.
CHEAM, a parish and village in Surrey, 2 m. NE by E of Ewell. Area 1,850 acres. Pop. 1,109.
CHEARSLEY, a parish in Bucks, 4 m. NNE of Thames. Area 1,130 acres. Pop. 308.

CHEAT, a river of Virginia, a branch of the Monongohela river. The upper part of its course lies in the Alleghany mountains, whose steep slopes in some places ascend from the edge of the stream at an angle of 40° or 50° to the height of 700 or 800 ft. It rises in several streams in the Pocahontas mountains, and flows nearly direct N to Kingwood, where it bends SW, and enters the Monongohela, on the r. bank, on the S frontier of Pennsylvania, after a course of 130 m.
CHEBOYGAN, a county in the N part of the S peninsula of Michigan, U. S., intersected by a river of the same name. Area 300 sq. m. CHE

CHEBSEY, a parish in Staffordshire, 21 m. E of Eccleshall, on the Sow river, intersected by the Grand-junction railway, which has a station at Norton-bridge in this p., 343 m. from Birmingham. Area 3.760 acres. Pop. 442.
CHEBUCTO. See Halifax, Nova Scotia.

CHECA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. of Guadalaxara, partido of Molina-de-Aragon, on the l. bank of the Cabrilles, at the foot of the Sierra-de-Molina. Pop. 1,200. There is an excellent iron-mine in the vicinity.
CHECACUPI, a town of Peru, in the dep. of Cuzco, near the r. bank of the Urabamba, 55 m. SE of Cuzco.
CHECHOKE, a village of Northern India, in Bahawulpur, near the l. bank of the Gura, in N lat. 30° 8'.
CHE-CHING-HYEN, a town of China, in the prov. of Honan, in N lat. 34° 8', long. 0° 57' W of Pekin.—Also a town in the prov. of Kwang-tung, in N lat. 21° 32', long. 6° 38' W of Pekin.
CHE-CHUEN-HYEN, a district of China, in the prov. of Honan, division of Nan-gan-fu.
CHE-CHU-FU, a town and district of China, in the prov. of Kiang-su, in N lat. 30° 45', long. 0° 58' E of Pekin.
CHECINY, or CHECZYNY, a town of Poland, in the gov. and circle of Kielce, on the Czarna, 10 m. SW of Kielce. Pop. 3,000, of whom a large proportion are Jews. Lead is mined in the vicinity.
CHECKAU, a fort in Afghanistan, on the route from Candahar to Herat, in N lat. 32° 34'.
CHECKENDON, a parish in Oxford, 71 m. S of Wallington. Area 2,800 acres. Pop. 398.
CHECKLEY-AND-TEAN, a parish in Stafford, 6 m. NW of Uttoxeter, on the Tean. Area 5,710 acres. Pop. 2,322. Tape is largely manufactured in this p.
CHECKLEY-WITH-WRINEHILL, a township in the p. of Wybunbury, Cheshire, 7 m. SE by E of Nantwich. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 213.

CHECO, a village of Chili, in the prov. of Coquimbo, in the partido of Copiapo. There are copper mines here which yield annually 6,000 quintals, and at former periods have given even 12,000 q. The ore contains about 70 per cent. of copper; but must be conveyed by troops of mules to La Punta, 10 leagues distant, where the smelting-furnaces are situated; and thence, on the backs of mules, to the port, which is 30 leagues distant.

CHECHUHI, or CHICUHI, a river which traces in part the frontier line between Brazil and Paraguay. It rises near Igatimi, receives the Iguarahi- Asu, and falls into the Paraguay on the l. bank in S lat. 24° 12'.
CHECY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. and 6 m. E of Orleans, near the Loire. Pop. 1,945.

CHEDEBUCTO BAY, an indentation of the coast of Nova Scotia, on the S of the gut of Canseau, entering from the Atlantic between Madame island and Cape Canseau. It is 25 m. in length, and from 10 to 12 m. in breadth. Its shores are a great resort of the herring and mackarel fishermen. At its head is a fine inlet called Milford haven, about 1 m. wide, and 12 or 15 m. in length.
CHEDBURGH, a parish in Suffolk, 81 m. NNE of Clare. Area 550 acres. Pop. 284.

CHEDDER, a parish and village in Somersetshire, 3 m. SE of Axbridge, at the foot of the Mendip hills. The p. has an area of 6,690 acres. Pop. 2,325. The rich pasturage of this p., which has the appearance of a continuous garden, has rendered its dairies long celebrated for their rich cheese.
CHEDDINGTON, a parish in Bucks, 2 m. NW of Ivinghoe. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 439.—Also a v. in the p. of Ivinghoe. Pop. 79.
CHEDDLETON, a parish in Stafford, 3 m. SW of Leek. Area 9,080 acres. Pop. 1,824.
CHEDDON-FITZPAINE, a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. NE of Ilchester. Area 1,050 acres. Pop. 357.
CHEDGRAVE, a parish in Norfolk, 7 m. NNW of Beccles, watered by the Thirn, a branch of the Yare. It forms a suburb of the town of Loddon. Area 900 acres. Pop. 348.
CHEDIAC. See SHEDIAC.
CHEDINGTON, or CHEDDINGTON, a parish of Dorset, 16 m. NW of Dorchester. Area 2,690 acres. Pop. 186.
CHEDISTON, a parish of Suffolk, 2 m. WNW of Halesworth. Area 1,910 acres. Pop. 433.
CHEDUAN. See SHEDUAN.

CHEDUBA, or Manaong, an island in the bay of Bengal, off the coast of Arracan, intersected by the parallel of 18° 48' N, and the meridian of 93° 40' E. It is about 40 m. in circumf., and is moderately elevated. It is thinly inhabited.
CHEDWORTH, a parish of Gloucestershire, 4 m. SW of Northleach. Area 4,890 acres. Pop. 983.
CHEDZOY, a parish of Somerset, 3 m. E of Bridgewater. Area 1,570 acres. Pop. 507.

CHEE, a river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of the Meuse, near the village of Seigneulles, cant. of Vavincourt; enters the dep. of the Marne; and after a course of about 39 m. unites with the Saulx, on the r. bank, above Vitry-le-Brulé.

CHE'E, a river of Abyssinia, separating Gojam- Proper, on the N, from Enabsie and Enassie, and flowing into the Abai at Mount Somma. It has a broad bed which is filled in the rainy season, but, from its rapid descent, is soon emptied.—Beke.
CHEESEBURN-GRANGE, a township of the p. of Standfordham, Northumberland, 111 m. N of Newcastle-upon-Tyne. Pop. 56.
CHEESEHILL (St. Peter), a parish of Southampton. Pop. 648.
CHEETHAM, a chapelry in the p. of Manchester, Lancashire, 2 m. NNW of Manchester. Area 1,110 acres. Pop. 6,082.
CHEF (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Isere, cant. of Bourgoin, 9 m. NW of La Tour-du-Pin. Pop. in 1841, 3,411 (offic. 3,401). The environs afford good wine.

CHEF-BOUTONNE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Deux-Sèvres, arrond. of Melle. The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,538; in 1841, 11,101. The town is situated near the source of the Boutonne, 10 m. SSE of Melle. Druggets and other woollen fabrics, earthenware, and leather, form the chief articles of local manufacture. Important cattle markets are held here.
CHEFE-CAJAN, a town of Mantchuria, in N lat. 47° 49′, long. 16° 11′ E of Pekin.
CHEFFES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Briollay, on the r. bank of the Sarthe. Pop. 1,311.
CHEGADA, a river in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, rising in the Serra Calimbao, flowing through the parish of Rio-Bonito, and joining the Tinguá.
CHE-GAN-SHU, a town of China, in the prov. of Sze-chuen, in N lat. 28° 30', long. 8° 57' W of Pekin.
CHEGONG, a village of Chinese Tartary, on the N bank of the Sutledge, in N lat. 31° 31', E long. 78° 5'.

CHEHARBAGH, a village of Afghanistan, 5 m. W of Jellalabad, in N lat. 34° 23'. The surrounding scenery is very beautiful. CHEHARDEH, [i. e. 'the Four villages,'] a village and district of Persia, in the prov. of Astrabad, between Astrabad and Cheshmesh-Ali. It contains, CHE as the name implies, four separate villages clustered together. Barley and wheat are cultivated in the environs, which are fertile.

CHEHEL-TAN, a mountain of Beluchistan, overhanging the town of Mustan on the S. Its alt. has not been estimated; but Masson, who ascended it, says, "it takes a long July's day to ascend it;" and the plain from which it rises has an elevation of from 4,000 to 5,000 ft. above sea-level. Snow does not remain on its summit beyond June or the beginning of July. The gradations in the alt. and temp. of this mountain are well marked by its vegetable zones.
CHE'HE'RY, a village of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. and 7 m. SE of Grandpré, near the r. bank of the Aire. Pop. 128.
CHE-HING-HYEN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Kwang-tung, division of Nan-heungchu.
CHEIBAKPOL, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 37 m. E of Grodno, district and 27 m. SW of Lida.
CHEILLE', a commune of France, in the dep. of the Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Azay-le-Rideau, near the l. bank of the Indre, 12 m. NE of Chinon. Pop. 1,310.
CHEIN-DESSUS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of Aspet. Pop. 1,131.
CHEIRAN, a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Yemen, 100 m. SSE of Sana, and 10 m. SW of Kataba.

CHEIRE, or CHEYRES, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 20 m. W of Freyburg, and 6 m. SW of Estavayer, on the r. bank of the lake of Neufchatel. Pop. (Cath.) 313. The locality affords good wine.
CHEITLAPHE-RENGHNAKOR-KANGEE, a town of Ultra-Gangetic India, in Tavoy, 15 m. NE of Amherst.
CHEIWAN, or Aasheyme, a town of Arabia, in the prov. of Yemen, 30 m. SW of Sada, on a hill. It is large, and was formerly the residence of the kings of Hamjar.
CHEK-CHU, a village of China, on the S side of the island of Hong-Kong. The pop., amounting to about 800, are chiefly employed in trading, farming, and curing fish.
CHE-KEANG, or CHE-KYANG, a district and town in the prov. of Hu-fu, division of King-chu-fu.
CHE-KEANG-HEEN, a district and town of China, in the prov. of Hu-nan, division of Yuen-chu-fu.
CHEKI, a town of Georgia, on an affluent of the Kur, 110 m. ESE of Tiflis.

CHEKSNA, a river of Russia, in the gov. of Novgorod, which, issuing from the SE side of Lake Bielo, flows in a winding but prevailingly S course to the Volga, which it joins a little above Mologa in the gov. of Jaroslav, after a course of about 240 m. Its principal affluents are the Souda, the Ouloma, the Slavenka, the Soukhona, the Pidma, the Sogoja, and the Oukhra. CHE-KYANG, CHE-KIANG, or TCHE-KIANG, a maritime province of China, lying between the parallels of 27° and 31° N lat., and, exclusive of its islands, the meridians of 118° and 121°. It is bounded on the N by the prov. of Kiang-su; on the E by the Eastern sea; on the S by the prov. of Fo-kien; and on the W by the prov. of Kiang-su and the Nganhoei or Ngan-hwai district of the prov. of Kyang-nan. Gutzlaff estimates its area at 39,150 sq. m.; and its pop. at 26,256,784. The surface of the country in the interior is generally hilly, with a reddish sandy surface; on the coast it is level, and the soil clayey. The whole is under high cultivation. Silk and tea are the staple articles of produce. The coasts are thickly studded with islands, among which the Chusan group is the most conspicuous. The rivers are numerous. The largest, the Chyang, or Tcheutang-chiang, has a NNE course, and appears to be | divided from the rivers flowing to the coast by a range of mountains crossing the country in a somewhat parallel course. Its cap. Hang-chu, Hangchow, or Hang-cheu, in N lat. 30° 20', E long. 120° 15', between the mouth of the Imperial canal and that of the Chyang, is, next to Pekin, the principal city in the empire. The other principal cities are Ning-po, Shin-hai, Cha-po, Shaou-hing-fo, and Shi-po. -The prov. is governed by a Fu-yuen, under the viceroy of Fo-kyen. It is divided into 11 fus or districts: viz. Hang-chu, Ning-po, Hu-chu, Kea-hing, Shaou-hing, Ta-chu, Kin-hwa, Ku-chu, Yeu-chu, Wan-chu, and Chu-chu.
CHELA-CHAP, a town on the S coast of the island of Java, in E long. 109°, to the W of the mouth of the Saraya river.
CHELAGSKOY, or SHELATSKOI (CAPE), a promontory of Russia in Asia, on the Arctic ocean, forming the NE extremity of Tchaounskai bay, in N lat. 70° 5′, and E long. 170° 30′.

CHELBOROUGH (East and West), two parishes in Dorsetshire. The former, 53 m. NE of Beaminster, has an area of 1,450 acres. Pop. 94. The latter, immediately adjacent on the W, has an area of 590 acres. Pop. 58.
CHELDON, a parish in Devon, 3 m. ESE of Chumleigh, on the S bank of the Dart. Area 1,610 acres. Pop. 90.
CHELEBI-HASSAN, a village of Anadolia, in the pash. and 50 m. SE of Sivas, on the road to Malatia.
CHELEBI-KOI, a village of Anadolia, in the sanjak of Kastamuni, in N lat. 41° 24', at an alt. of 2,780 ft. above sea-level.
CHELEL, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Wilna, 50 m. W by N of Rossiena.
CHELES, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Estremadura, 28 m. SW of Badajoz, near the l. bank of the Guadiana. Pop. 1,000.
CHELFORD, a chapelry in Cheshire, 7 m. W of Macclesfield, intersected by the Manchester and Birmingham railway. Area 290 acres. Pop. 201.
CHELICUT, a village of Abyssinia, in the plain of Antalo, prov. of Tigre, in N lat. 13° 21' 34", E long. 40° 37′ 17″ [Salt].
CHELIF. See SHELLIF.
CHELIGOFF (LAKE), a large lake in Russian America, in N lat. 59°, W long. 155°, which discharges its waters into the head of Bristol bay.
CHELIGOFF (STRAIT OF), a channel between the island of Kodiak and the peninsula of Alashka, in Russian America.
CHELIDONI. See KHELIDONIA.

CHELINDREH, or GULNARE, a small port of Turkey in Asia, pash. of Itshili, on the coast, 30 m. ENE of Cape Anamur, 130 m. SSE of Konieh. It consists of a custom-house and three or four huts; but has some importance from its being the port through which the Turkish government generally transmits its despatches to Cyprus. It perhaps occupies the site of the ancient Melania.
CHELL, a township in the p. of Wolstanton in Staffordshire, 4 m. NNE of Newcastle-under-Lyne. Pop. 737.
CHELLASTON, a parish in Derbyshire, 5 m. SSE of Derby. Area 810 acres. Pop. 461.
CHELLES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Lagny, 12 m. SW of Meaux. Pop. 1,632.
CHELLESWORTH, or CHELSWORTH, a parish in Suffolk, 1 m. SW of Bildeston. Area 610 acres. Pop. 284.
CHELLINGTON, a parish in Bedfordshire, 6 m. NNE of Olney. Area 610 acres. Pop. 125. CHE

CHELM, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Lublin, circle and 18 m. NE of Krasnystaw, on the r. bank of the Uher, in N lat. 51° 7'. Pop. 2,900, of whom the greater part are Jews. It is the seat of a Greek bishop. The Poles were defeated here by the Russians, on the 8th of June, 1794.
CHELMARSH, a parish in Salop, 4 m. SE of Bridgnorth, on the Severn. Area 3,190 acres. Pop. 495.

CHELMER, a river of Essex, which rises near Debden, and runs from NNW to SSE to Chelmsford, where it receives the Can, a stream of about 14 m. length of course, on the r. bank; it then turns E and receives the Ter, a stream of 14 m. length of course, on the l. bank, and falls into the Blackwater near Maldon, after a course of 35 m.
CHELMERTON, a chapelry in the p. of Bakewell, in Derbyshire, 5 m. SW of Tideswell. Pop. 238.
CHELMO, a town of Greece, in the Morea, on one of the head-streams of the Carbonaro, 7 m. SE of Leondari.
CHELMONDISTON, a parish in Suffolk, 6 m. SSE of Ipswich, near the estuary of the Orwell. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. 564.
CHELMOS, a mountain of Greece, in the Morea, forming the W portion of the range which encloses the bed of the Eure or Vasilico on the N and NE.

CHELMSFORD, a parish and a county town in Essex, near the centre of the co., at the confluence of the Chelmer and the Can. Area of p. 1,750 acres. Pop. including the hamlet of Moulsham, 6,789.—The town chiefly consists of three streets, in which are several good houses. The shire-hall, an elegant structure, occupies the centre of the town. A fine iron bridge crosses the Chelmer; a stone bridge of one arch thrown across the Can connects the town with the populous suburb of Moulsham; and the Eastern Counties railway is conducted across the Can on a viaduct of 18 arches of 30 ft. span. About 1 m. from the town is the county jail, built on the radiating principle.
CHELMSFORD, a township in Middlesex co., in Massachusetts, U. S., 27 m. NNW of Boston, on the S side of Merrimac river. Pop. 1,697.

CHELO'NA, a mountain-chain on the N frontier of Greece, the most prominent and remarkable of the upper chain which encircles the basin of the gulf of Arta. It slopes abruptly to the eastward into the valley of the Aspro.
CHELQUE, a town of Chili, in Araucana, on a head-stream of the Cruces, 60 m. WNW of Valdivia.

CHELSEA, a parish in the Kensington division of the hund. of Ossulstone, Middlesex, a suburb of London, on the N side of the Thames, at a broad part of the river, over which there is a bridge connecting it with the p. of Battersea. Area of p. 780 acres. Inhabited houses in 1831, 4,635; in 1841, 5,648. Pop. in 1801, 11,604; in 1831, 32,371; in 1841, 40,179. From the beauty of its situation, and its vicinity to the metropolis; this suburb was long ago selected by the citizens of London as a place of retirement from the fatigues of business or of dissipation; and the splendid mansions here reared by those who courted temporary repose in its solitude justified its appellation of 'the village of palaces.' Here Sir Thomas More, the witty, the upright, but bigotted chancellor of Henry VIII., resided; here was the princely dwelling of the bishops of Winchester; here the good Queen Bess had a favourite palace; and here Sir Robert Walpole spent some of his years 'unclogged with cares of state.' The buildings which sheltered these great men and women of other ages have all successively disappeared; but have been replaced by others not less worthy of admiration, though tenanted it may be by less ambitious spirits. Ranelagh also stood in this parish; but its glories are faded, and its once splendid walls are desolate and deserted. There are many elegant houses in Cheyné walk in the old town; and in the new, a fine line of houses, named Sloanestreet, extends nearly 1 m. in length. The buildings of C. are situated on the widest reach of the Thames westward of London bridge. A chain-pier of handsome construction has been erected on the mall of Cheyné walk, for the accommodation of the riversteamers. The old church, now used as a chapel, is a small brick edifice by no means conspicuous for its beauty. It contains many curious monuments, especially one to Sir Thomas More, whose body, it is most probable, was interred within the Tower after his execution. The new church, a magnificent and spacious edifice, was built in 1824 at an expense of £40,000. It is in the pointed style of architecture, with a lofty square tower. The C. water works are under the management of a company incorporated in 1724. In 1767 the daily supply raised by their works amounted to 1,740 tuns; it was shortly after increased to 3,500; in 1809 it exceeded 6,500; and it may now amount to 20,000 tuns.—The apothecaries' garden, on the margin of the Thames, comprises between 3 and 4 acres. To the garden are attached a hot-house, green-houses, and a library of natural history. A second botanic garden in Sloane-street, of considerably larger extent, well stocked with plants, arranged after the Linnæan system, was founded by Mr. William Curtis, well known by his two great works, the 'Flora Londinensis,' and the 'Botanical Magazine.' The grounds comprise rather more than 6 acres, and are disposed with much taste. In several other parts of this parish are large nursery gardens. The Royal hospital.] The most remarkable object in C. is the national asylum for maimed and worn-out soldiers, the Royal military hospital, one of the noblest foundations in the world, the buildings of which alone were finished at an expense of £150,000. The building is of brick, in the form of a parallelogram, ornamented with freestone quoins, cornices, pediments, and columns, It is solid, commanding, and of elevated proportions; and, in general architectural character, is judiciously suited to its object. It stands at a short distance from the river. Large gardens extend to the edge of the water, and finish with an elevated terrace. To the N is a field of 13 acres, laid out in walks, with avenues of trees for a promenade. The building consists of three courts, the principal of which is open on the S side towards the river, and is ornamented, in the centre, with a bronze statue of Charles II. The two wings are each 365 feet in length, and consist chiefly of the pensioners' wards. The whole length from E to W is 790 ft. The care of this institution is vested in the lord-president of the council, the first lord of the treasury, the secretaries - of - state, the paymaster - general of the forces, the secretary - at - war, the comptroller of army - accounts, and the governor and the lieutenant-governor of the royal hospital. The establishment consists of a governor, secretary, two chaplains, a physician, surgeon, and apothecary, a comptroller, steward, clerk of the works, and other subordinate warrant-officers. The number of in-pensioners is usually about 500. They are provided with clothes, lodging, and diet, and an allowance, varying according to rank and service, from 8d. to 3s. 6d. per week. The allowance to the out-pensioners, or those who live in dwellings of their own throughout the country, is from 5d. to 3s. 6d. a-day. Their number is not limited by law, and in 1836-7 amounted to 79,500, their annual expense being £1,359,463. The expense of the hospital is chiefly defrayed by an annual grant from the parliament, voted with the army estimates. Military asylum.] The royal military asylum or hospital in this p. was founded in 1801 by parliamentary grant. Its objects are to support and educate the orphan children of soldiers and non-commissioned officers, and the children of those who are on foreign stations. It is upheld by parliamentary grants, and by the annual contribution of one day's pay from the whole army. The building is on an extensive plan, and is chiefly formed of brick, with embellishments of stone. The principal parts compose three sides of a quadrangle; and the W or chief front has, in the centre, a portico of the Doric order. The official establishment consists of a commandant, an adjutant and secretary, a chaplain, quarter-master, surgeon, matron, and various subordinate persons. In 1850 this institution contained about 400 boys, varying in age from 5 to 14, who are taught reading, writing, and the more useful branches of arithmetic; besides which, from the age of 11, they are employed, five hours a-day in summer, and four hours a-day in winter, on alternate days, in works | CHE of industry; and, in this short period, they make every article of clothing required for their own use. On parade days, the boys, dressed in military uniform, with flags flying, drums beating, and full band playing, proceed to the parade, where they perform various military evolutions, and go through a course of gymnastic exercises. Attached to the asylum is a training school for schoolmasters for the army. It is yet in its infancy, but is likely to become of importance. There is also a training college, called St. Mark's college, for schoolmasters, founded by the National Society at Stanley-grove, in the W extremity of the p. It is intended to consist of 100 students, the charge for each youth being £25 per annum.
CHELSEA, a township and town in Orange co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 24 m. SE of Montpelier. Pop. 1,959.—Also a township in Suffolk co., in Massachusetts, 4 m. NE of Boston. Pop. 2,290.
CHELSFIELD, a parish in Kent, 63 m. SE of Bromley. Area 3,330 acres. Pop. 861.
CHELSHAM, a parish in Surrey, 61 m. SE of Croydon. Area 2,700 acres. Pop. 347.

CHELTENHAM, a market-town and parish in the county of Gloucester, 96 m. NW of London; by the Great Western railway, 121 m. Area of p. 3,740 acres. Pop. in 1801, 3,076; in 1831, 22,942; in 1841, 31,411. The town is delightfully situated in an extensive and fertile valley, open to the S and W, but on the N, E, and SE sheltered and its temp. rendered agreeable by the Malvern hills, and by the immense amphitheatre formed by the Cotswold ridge meeting with the Leckhampton hills. These latter terminate abruptly about 2 m. to the SE of the town. which consists principally of one noble street called the High Street, upwards of 1 m. in length. Branching off from this main street, numerous new streets have been formed, which display many elegant and tasteful varieties of architectural decoration; and a number of detached villas adorn the suburbs and the surrounding country. Altogether Cheltenham is a very agreeable residence; and one of the most fashionable towns in the kingdom.—The town derives its importance and celebrity solely from its mineral springs, the first of which—the Spring or Spa, as it was long called by way of distinction—was discovered by accident in the year 1716. Several other mineral springs have, at different periods, been since discovered, possessing, in varied proportions, chalybeate, aperient neutral salts, chiefly sulphate of soda, sulphate of magnesia, and oxide of iron, held in solution by carbonic acid and iodine. - C. is entirely dependent on its visitors, who are generally invalids of the more affluent classes. Its convenient situation, between the Cotswold hills and the vale of Gloucester, renders it a considerable mart for sheep and corn. Malting was once its chief business. Coal is brought by the Severn to Gloucester, from Staffordshire, from Shropshire, and the Forest of Dean, and thence by railroad to the wharfs at the W end of the town.—C. was enfranchised by the Reform act, and now returns one member to parliament. The number of electors in 1847 was 2,345. The boundaries of the parish and borough are usually represented as coinciding.
CHELTENHAM, a township in Montgomery co., in Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. 1,053.
CHELTIK, or CHELTIJAK, a village of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. of Sivas, 7 m. NW of Vezir- Köpri, near the r. bank of the Kizil-Irmak.

CHELVA, a town of Spain, cap. of a judicial partido in the prov. and 40 m. NW of Valencia, on the l. bank of a river of the same name, an affluent of the Guadalavier. Pop. 4,264. It is well-built, and contains the ruins of a Roman aqueduct. The partido comprises 23 pueblos. It affords wine, oil, and silk, in considerable quantities.
CHELVESTON, or CHELSTON, a parish of Northampton, 2 m. NE of Higham-Ferrers. Area 1,730 acres. Pop., with hamlet of Caldicot, 372.
CHELVEY, a parish of Somerset, 81 m. WSW of Bristol, intersected by the Bristol and Exeter railway. Area 320 acres. Pop. 54.
CHELWOOD, a parish of Somerset, 2 m. SE of Pensford. Area 1,130 acres. Pop. 260.

CHELY (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lozere, arrond. of Marvejols. The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,476; in 1841, 5,545. The town, called also Saint- Chely-Ville, or Saint-Chely-d'Apcher, is situated in a mountainous district, 19 m. N of Marvejols. Pop. in 1846, 1,582. It contains manufactories of woollen fabrics, leather, and parchment; and has an active trade in horses, mules, cattle, sheep, pigs, wool, grain, dried legumes, potatoes, chestnuts, cheese, and articles of local manufacture. Fairs are held seven times a-year.

CHILY - D'AUBRAC (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, arrond. of Espalion. The cant. comprises only the com. of the same name. Pop. in 1831, 3,289, in 1841, 3,252. The town is on the r. bank of the Boralde, 11 m. NE of Espalion. Pop. in 1846. 2,088. It has a considerable trade in cattle, and tour annual fairs.
CHELY-DU-TARN (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Lozere, cant. of Sainte-Enimie. Pop. 577.
CHEMALI, a village of Asia Minor, in Anadolia, in the sanjak and 93 m. WSW of Biga, and about 6 m. from the coast of the Mediterranean. It is supposed to occupy the site of the ancient Colone.
CHEMAZE', a commune and town of France, in the cant. and 4 m. SW of Château-Gontier. Pop. 1,586.
CHE'ME'RE'-LE-ROI, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Meslay, 19 m. ESE of Laval, on the l. bank of the Erve. Pop. 1,172.

CHEMILLE', a canton, commune, and town of France in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, arrond. of Beaupreau. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,283; in 1841, 13,997. The town, which is small but of great antiquity, is situated on the Hyrome, 15 m. E of Beaupreau. Pop. in 1841, 4,049. It contains several paper-mills, extensive manufactories and bleacheries of calico, linen, handkerchiefs, &c., and has a considerable trade in cattle. Fairs are held eight times a-year.—Also a village in the dep., and on the l. bank of the Allier, cant. of Souvigny, 6 m. S of Moulins. Pop. 640.
CHEMILLE'-LE-BLANC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Neuvy-le- Roi, 21 m. N of Tours. Pop. 1,314.
CHEMIN, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Jura, arrond. of Dôle. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,623; in 1841, 8,741. The town is 13 m. SW of Dôle. Pop. in 1846, 448.
CHEMINON, or CHEMINON-LA-VILLE-ET-L'ABBAYE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Marne, cant. of Thieblemont, 16 m. E of Vitry-le- Francais. Pop. 1,239.
CHEMINE' - EN - CHARNIE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Loire, 21 m. W of Le Mans. Pop. 949. It contains several blast furnaces and forges.
CHEMIRE'-LE-GAUDIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of La Suze, 14 m. SW of Le Mans. Pop. 1,680.
CHEMNITZ, or-as it is now pronounced-Kem-

NITZ, a bailiwick and town of Saxony, in the gov. of Zwickau, 37 m. WSW of Dresden, 15 m. E of Penig on the Mulde, and 20 m. ENE of Zwickau, on a stream of the same name, an affluent of the Mulde II. CHE on the r., and at an alt. of 944 ft. above sea-level. It is a clean, well-built town; with spacious and straight streets; and several very handsome public buildings. But its chief importance is derived from its extensive cotton and woollen manufactures. Next to Vienna and Elberfeld, C. is the most important seat of this manufacture in Germany. In 1832, it possessed 8 cotton spinning-mills with 22,912 spindles; in 1835, these had increased to 17 mills, with 70,000 spindles; but the increase has been much less rapid since that period. It has also woollen spinning-mills and silk-mills; and manufactures steam-engines and machinery to a considerable annual amount. Hosiery is extensively manufactured; but the woollen manufactures of C. have decreased considerably since the middle of last century. There are numerous dyeing and bleaching establishments; and several extensive cotton-printing works. The number of inhabited houses in 1837 was 1,163; of inhabitants, 22,265. The present pop. is about 30,000.
CHEMNITZ. See KAMNITZ.

CHEMUNG, a county of the state of New York, U. S., comprising an area of 530 sq. m., watered by a river of the same name, Cayuta, and Newtown creeks, and intersected by the Chemung canalwhich connects Seneca lake with the C. river-and by the New York and Erie railroad. The surface is hilly, but the soil is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 20,732. Cap. Elmira. The river C., formed by the junction of the Conhocton with Tioga river, in Steuben co., flows SE, and enters the Susquehanna at Athens, in the state of Pennsylvania. - Also a township of Chemung co., in the state of New York, 10 m. SE of Elmira, intersected in the S by the river of the same name, and by the New York and Erie railroad. The surface is partly hilly, and the soil gravelly loam and alluvium. Pop. 2,377. CHENAB [Burnes], or CHUNAB [Elphinstone and Vigne], a river of Northern India, the largest of the five great rivers of the Punjab. It rises in Middle Tibet, under the name of the Chandra, in a small lake called Chundra-Bagha [Vigne]; and flows W towards the pass of Rutunka to the N of the source of the Beas. Flowing through this pass, the estimated alt. of which is 13,000 ft., it turns NW, and flows in that direction, between two parallel ridges of the Himalaya, till it intersects the meridian of 76°, where it turns W to Kishtawar. At Tandi or Tandeo, 20 m. beyond the Rutunka pass, it receives the Suruj-Bhaga from the N, and becomes a stream of about 200 ft. broad, with a full steady current, and a volume of water gradually augmented by tributaries from the mountain-chains on each side. At a point, 2 m. N of Kishtawar, which is in N lat. 33° 15', E long. 75° 45', it receives the Musuwardwun from the N, and turns suddenly to the S. At this point it flows in a deep fissure, between rocks of gneiss and slate, which is crossed by a swing-bridge only 25 yards in length. Vigne estimates the alt. of Kishtawar above sea-level at 5,150 ft. He crossed the river at a point 7 m. S of Kishtawar, by a lever bridge of bold construction thrown across the rocky chasm, about 70 yards in width, through which the river flows. At Badrawar the river again turns to the W, and maintains this direction to Harawug, in N lat. 33° 1', E long. 75° 7', where it receives a considerable tributary descending through the Banitral pass. From Harawug, the C. flows in a SW course, to a point about 15 m. W of Jamee, passing Rebursi, where it enters the lower plains of the Punjab with a deep stream of 200 yards wide, and Aknur, about 50 m. lower down, in the open plains of the Punjab, where it becomes navigable for timber-rafts. From a point about 16 m. S of Aknur, its course is mainly W to Vazirabad, where Macartney found it, in the month of June, flowing with a current of 5 m. per hour, a depth of 14 ft., and a breadth of 1 m. 3 furl. 30 perches. Its principal tributary, in this section of its course, is the Perhamgala from the N. From Vazirabad, its course is WSW to near Ramnaggur. At the point where it passes on the N of this town, it must, says Hough, be more than 1 m. broad in the rains, and have a depth of from 14 to 16 ft. of water; but he found it flowing in three fordable streams, 1 m. below that town, with a depth of only 3 ft. of water in the middle one, on the 17th of December. Mohun Lal says the river is 200 yards wide at the ferry near Rarmal-above Ramnaggur; but that in the rainy season it overflows the country for 2 m. on both sides. From a point about 20 m. to the W of Ramnaggur, the C. pursues a SW course to its confluence with the Jelum, in N lat. 31° 11′ 30″, E long. 72° 10′, 45 m. N of Tolumba. The junction of these two great rivers, the Acesines and Hydaspes of antiquity, is described in highly exaggerated terms by Arrian. Burnes, who visited the point of confluence in the month of June, when the waters must have been near their highest, found them meeting with "a murmuring noise" but gentle current; nor are there any eddies or rocks to render the navigation perilous. The C., 50 m. below the confluence of the Jelum, is joined by the Ravi, which throws itself into it by three canal-like mouths. At the point of union, the C. had a breadth of 3 of a m. on the 23d of June, but its deep part did not extend over 500 yards. [Burnes.] Continuing its course SW, the C., 110 m. below the confluence of the Jelum, receives the Ghara or Sutledge; but appeared to Burnes "quite as large above as below the conflux." Mohun Lal found the river, 10 m. below Multan, on the 4th Feb., flowing with a breadth of 350 yards, in "a current not rapid," and with a depth of nearly 14 fath. The united five streams, under the name of the Punjnud, join the Indus on the l. bank, 3 m. below Mittunkore, in N lat. 28° 55', E long. 70° 28′, 470 m. above the mouth of that great river.—Burnes' Travels in Bokhara.—Vigne's Travels in Kashmir.

CHENANGO, a county of the state of New York, comprising an area of 800 sq. m., watered by a river of the same name and its tributaries, and Unadilla river, and intersected by Chenango canal, which connects the Susquehanna with the Erie canal. It presents a hilly surface; but its soil, especially in the valleys, is extremely fertile. Pop. in 1830, 37,238; in 1840, 40,785. Cap., Norwich.—The river C. rises in Madison and Oneida co., and falls, after a course of 75 m., into the Susquehanna at Binghamton. - Also a township of Broome co., in the State of New York, 145 m. SW of Albany. Its surface is hilly, and is watered by the C. and Susquehanna rivers; its soil, especially on the rivers, is highly productive. Pop. in 1840, 5,465.—Also a township of Beaver co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 238 m. NW of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,435.

CHENANGO FORKS, a village of Chenango township, Broome co., in the state of New York, 127 m. WSW of Albany, at the junction of the Tioughnioga and C. rivers, and on the C. canal. Pop. in 1840, about 500.
